publisher.publisher-name: Japanese Association for Laboratory Animal Science

title-group.article-title: Cardiorenal damages in mice at early phase after intervention induced by angiotensin II, nephrectomy, and salt intake

contrib-group.aff: 1)Life Science Center for Survival Dynamics, Tsukuba Advanced Research Alliance (TARA), University of Tsukuba, 1-1-1 Tennodai, Tsukuba Science City, Ibaraki 305-8577, Japan
2)Doctoral Program in Life and Agricultural Sciences, Graduate School of Life and Environmental Sciences, University of Tsukuba, 1-1-1 Tennodai, Tsukuba Science City, Ibaraki 305-8577, Japan
3)Doctoral Program in Medical Sciences, Graduate School of Comprehensive Human Sciences, University of Tsukuba, 1-1-1 Tennodai, Tsukuba Science City, Ibaraki 305-8575, Japan
4)Master’s Program in Agro-Bioresources Sciences and Technology, Graduate School of Science and Technology, University of Tsukuba, 1-1-1 Tennodai, Tsukuba Science City, Ibaraki 305-8572, Japan
5)Department of Nephrology, Faculty of Medicine, University of Tsukuba, 1-1-1 Tennodai, Tsukuba Science City, Ibaraki 305-8575, Japan
6)AMED-CREST, Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development, 1-7-1 Otemachi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo 100-0004, Japan

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: A. Fukamizu. email: akif@tara.tsukuba.ac.jp

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2024 Japanese Association for Laboratory Animal Science

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: The interconnection of heart performance and kidney function plays an important role for maintaining homeostasis through a variety of physiological crosstalk between these organs. It has been suggested that acute or chronic dysfunction in one organ causes dysregulation in another one, like patients with cardiorenal syndrome. Despite its growing recognition as global health issues, still little is known on pathophysiological evaluation between the two organs. Previously, we established a preclinical murine model with cardiac hypertrophy and fibrosis, and impaired kidney function with renal enlargement and increased urinary albumin levels induced by co-treatment with vasopressor angiotensin II (A), unilateral nephrectomy (N), and salt loading (S) (defined as ANS treatment) for 4 weeks. However, how both tissues, heart and kidney, are initially affected by ANS treatment during the progression of tissue damages remains to be determined. Here, at one week after ANS treatment, we found that cardiac function in ANS-treated mice (ANS mice) are sustained despite hypertrophy. On the other hand, kidney dysfunction is evident in ANS mice, associated with high blood pressure, enlarged glomeruli, increased levels of urinary albumin and urinary neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin, and reduced creatinine clearance. Our results suggest that cardiorenal tissues become damaged at one week after ANS treatment and that ANS mice are useful as a model causing transition from early to late-stage damages of cardiorenal tissues.

kwd-group.kwd: animal models for cardiorenal damages ANS mice
pathogenesis at early phase of cardiorenal damage

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Functional performance of heart and kidney is closely interconnected through a variety of physiological pathways including hemodynamic and vasoactive actions [1]. Of these homeostatic factors involved in crosstalk between distant organs, angiotensin II (Ang II), a vasoconstrictive hormone and a component of the renin-angiotensin system (RAS), plays an essential role in the regulation of blood pressure and electrolyte balance [2], but clinical studies have suggested that Ang II is one of factors inducing cardiorenal syndrome (CRS) [3,4,5].

CRS is the term explaining the conditions that represent an important model for investigating the pathophysiology of heart and kidney dysfunction [6, 7]. Deciphering of cardiorenal interaction is of major interest, for examples, regarding a primary failing organ, being unidirectional or bidirectional, the pathophysiological mechanism, and the time course of acute versus chronic disease development [8]. Although it is uncertain how cardiorenal tissues are primarily affected in clinical cases with chronic heart and kidney diseases, increased information concerning mouse models [9, 10] gives us a clue to consider what factors and tissues influential for distant organ dysfunction in the disease development.

Using a mouse model treated with chronic infusion of angiotensin II (A), unilateral nephrectomy (N), and high salt loading (S) which activate RAS, we have recently revealed cardiorenal dysfunction with reduced left ventricular fractional shortening of the heart, and with lowered glomerular filtration rate and increased albuminuria of the kidney, four weeks after ANS treatment [11]. In these experimental conditions, we also found increased levels of plasma histamine in ANS mice, implying its protective effect on the development of cardiorenal damages. In fact, the transcriptome analysis demonstrated that a histamine agonist has a role for anti-inflammatory action to protect cardiorenal dysfunction [11]. However, it remains unsolved when heart and kidney in mice are primarily influenced by ANS treatment in the damage progression.

In the present study, one week after ANS treatment, we have found that kidney dysfunction is evident, associated with increased diameter of glomeruli, reduced creatinine clearance, and increased urinary albumin, and shown that cardiac function is maintained because of no change in fractional shortening at this point, although cardiac hypertrophy is observed.

sec.title: Animal experiments
sec.p: All animal experiments in this study were carried out humanely after approval from the Institutional Animal Experiment Committee of the University of Tsukuba. Experiments were performed in accordance with the Regulation of Animal Experiments of the University of Tsukuba, and the Fundamental Guidelines for Proper Conduct of Animal Experiments and Related Activities in Academic Research Institutions under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology of Japan. Mice were farmed in the Life Science Center for Survival Dynamics, Tsukuba Advanced Research Alliance (TARA)-SPF space, in an environment of 22°C, humidity of 40–60%, and light and dark cycle every 12 h. Eight- to ten-weeks old C57BL/6J male mice were purchased from CLEA Japan, Inc. (Tokyo, Japan), and used for generating ANS mice.

sec.title: Cardiorenal damage mice with ANS
sec.p:
Mice with cardiorenal damage induced by ANS were prepared as describe previously [1, 2, 11] (Fig. 1AFig. 1.
Changes in the physiological parameters in ANS mice at 1 week after treatment. (A) Experimental design of ANS-treated mice. Time dependent changes in (B) Body weight (n=22–24), (C) systolic blood pressure (n=7–10), (D) water intake (n=8–10) and (E) urine volume (n=8–10). Data are shown as means ± SEM. Statistical differences were determined using Student’s t-test or Welch’s t-test (ns, not significant; **, P<0.01, ***, P<0.001 as compared to sham mice). UNX: unilateral nephrectomy. Sham: control mice that underwent sham surgery by exposing the left kidney without removal.). Briefly, to perform uninephrectomy, male mice approximately 8- to 10-weeks old were anesthetized with mixed anesthetic agents (medetomidine, midazolam, and butorphanol). The left kidney was removed through a flank incision with unilateral nephrectomy (UNX). Control mice underwent sham surgery by exposing the left kidney without removal. Mice were subcutaneously infused with Angiotensin II peptide (#4001l; Peptide Institute Inc., Tokyo, Japan) at 1.2 mg/kg/day for 7 days by osmotic mini-pumps (#1007D; Alzet Osmotic Pumps, Cupertino, CA, USA) immediately after the uninephrectomy surgery. After the procedures, mice were awakened by administration of atipamezole, then ANS-treated mice were given drinking water containing 0.9% NaCl (Otsuka pharmaceutical Inc., Tokyo, Japan). Sham mice as controls using osmotic mini-pump infused with saline were given drinking water without NaCl. All mice had free access to commercial chow (MF diet; Oriental Yeast Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The diet and drinking water were provided ad libitum throughout the experiment.

sec.title: Sampling materials
sec.p: Echocardiography, urine collection, and blood pressure measurement were performed before the ANS treatment, and at 1 week after the treatment. The sample materials such as plasma, heart and kidney tissue were collected under general anesthesia by using the mixed anesthetic agents.

sec.title: Physiological analysis
sec.p: Systolic blood pressure and heart rate were measured in conscious mice by the tail-cuff method (BP-98a, Softron, Tokyo, Japan) as described previously [12, 13]. Echocardiography was performed with a Vevo 2100 High-Resolution Imaging System (Visual Sonics Inc., Toronto, Canada) equipped with a 40-MHz transducer, and measured before the ANS treatment and at 1 week after the treatment. Mice were anesthetized using isoflurane for animal inhalation (Zoetis Japan, Tokyo, Japan) adjusting the depth of anesthesia to keep the heart rate between 400 to 600 bpm. Short-axis M-mode images were obtained at the papillary muscle level. After measurement, software analysis was performed to calculate the left ventricular inner diameter contraction ratio (percentage left ventricular fractional shortening; FS) and thickness of left ventricular anterior wall at diastole (LVAW;d). The calculations were performed on three different points in time of each mouse.

sec.title: Urine collection
sec.p: Urine was collected for 3 days before the ANS treatment and at 1 week after the treatment from mice using metabolic cages. Urine 24-h volume and volume of drinking water were measured, and then 1 ml of the urine was collected. The dispensed urine was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm, then urine samples were collected and stored at −80°C until use.

sec.title: Kidney function analysis
sec.p: For the functional analysis of the kidney, creatinine clearance (Ccr) was measured at an indicator of glomerular filtration rate at 1 week after ANS treatment. Urinary creatinine was quantified by creatinine test kit (#290–65901, FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Co., Osaka, Japan) at 1 week after ANS treatment. Plasma creatinine was measured using Fuji Dri-Chem System (FDC 7000V, FUJIFILM Co., Tokyo, Japan). Ccr was calculated from plasma creatinine (Scr), urinary creatine (Ucr), Urine volume (Uv), and body surface area (BSA) using the following reduction formula. 9.822 were applied as the Meeh constant k for C57BL/6J strain [14, 15].Body surface Area (m2) = kBW (g)0.667

The urinary albumin was measured for each urine sample at the point before the ANS treatment and at 1 week after treatment by Rebis albumin mouse ELISA kit (#AKRAL-121, FUJIFILM Wako Shibayagi Co., Gunma, Japan), and the daily excretion of urinary albumin was calculated from the urinary albumin concentration and urine volume. The urinary neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL), as a diagnostic and prognostic marker for acute kidney injury (AKI), was measured for each urine sample at the time point before the ANS treatment, and at 1 week after the treatment. All assays were conducted using a Mouse Liocalin-2/NGAL Quantikine ELISA kit (#MLCN20, R&D Systems, Inc., Mineapolis, MN, USA) according to the instructions of the manufacturer. The urine from 100-fold diluted and 50 µl of each sample were used for quantification of NGAL level. The daily excretion of NGAL was calculated from the urinary NGAL concentration and urine volume. The cystatin C in plasma was measured by the Mouse/Rat Cystatin C Quantikine ELISA kit (#MSCTC0, R&D Systems, Inc., MN, USA) according to the manufactures instructions.

sec.title: Histological analysis
sec.p: The heart and kidney were harvested after perfusion fixation by 10% formalin neutral buffer solution under general anesthesia using the mixed anesthetic agents, then organs were fixed in 10% formalin neutral buffer solution for 48 h at 4°C, washed with PBS, dehydrated, and embedded in paraffin. Tissues were sectioned into 5 µm thickness for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, 3 µm for Masson’s Trichrome staining, 2 µm for Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining using a rotary microtome (HM430E, Microm International GmbH, Walldorf, Germany). After deparaffinization, to observe the structural changes in heart, H&E and Masson’s Trichrome-stained cardiac sections were prepared by standard procedure. Renal sections were stained with PAS and H&E to observe the pathological changes in tubules, glomeruli, and vessels by standard procedure. Data images were obtained using BX53 and SZ61 microscope with DP21 digital camera (Olympus, Tokyo, Japan). Four mice per each group were used for histological analysis. Diameter of glomeruli in kidneys obtained from sham and ANS mice were analyzed. Briefly, image of 30 to 50 glomeruli from renal PAS-stained section of each mouse, diameter was measured using ImageJ 1.52 K software (http://imagej.nih.gov/ij/).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 9 for Mac (Graphpad Software Co., La Jolla, CA, USA). The number of animals is shown in each figure legend. The Mann-Whitny U test, Welch’s t-test, Student’s t-test were used. Results with P<0.05 were considered statistically significant.

sec.title: Changes in the physiological parameters in ANS mice at 1 week after treatment
sec.p: To evaluate organ damages caused by ANS treatment in the early stage at 1 week after operation (Fig. 1A), we measured the physiological parameters, body weight, blood pressure, water intake, and urine volume, in ANS mice. The body weight gain of ANS group was suppressed compared with that in Sham group (Fig. 1B) and blood pressure of ANS group was significantly elevated (Fig. 1C). We also confirmed increased water intake (Fig. 1D) and urine volume (Fig. 1E) in ANS mice compared with those in Sham group.

sec.title: Cardiac morphology and function in ANS mice at 1 week after treatment
sec.p:
To observe cardiac morphology and function of ANS mice, we prepared H&E and PAS-stained sections, and measure heart weight and heart rates. We found that hearts from ANS were enlarged (Fig. 2AFig. 2.
Cardiac morphology and function in ANS mice at 1 week after treatment. (A) Appearance of heart from sham and ANS mice after 1 week of treatment. Scale bar represent 5 mm. Progressive exacerbation of (B) Cardiac hypertrophy (n=18–20) and increased heart rate (C) (n=7–10). (D) Whole heart images stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) (left) and Masson’s trichrome (right). Scale bars represent 1 mm (top) for low magnification, and 200 µm (bottom) for high magnification in (D). (E) Representative images of an echocardiographic assessment of Sham and ANS treated mice 1 week after treatment. Scalebar represent 1 mm for depth and 25 msec for time axis. (F) Wall thickness of anterior wall of left ventricle at diastole (n=9–10) and (G) cardiac function (n=9–10) evaluated by echocardiography. Data are shown as means ± SEM. Statistical differences were determined using Student’s t-test, Welch’s t-test or Mann-Whitney U test (ns, not significant; *, P<0.05; **, P<0.01; ****, P<0.0001 as compared to sham mice).) with increased heart weight (Fig. 2B) and heart rates (Fig. 2C) compared with Sham group at 1 week after ANS treatment. The H&E staining and the Masson’s Trichrome staining of heart from ANS-treated group showed remarkable cardiac hypertrophy (Fig. 2D, left panel) and ventricular interstitial fibrosis (Fig. 2D, right panel), respectively. Echocardiographic examination revealed the significant increase in the left ventricular anterior wall (LVAW) in ANS-treated group (Figs. 2E and F). On the other hand, cardiac contractility in ANS-treated group was preserved at 1 week after treatment (Fig. 2G).

sec.title: Impaired kidney function in ANS group at 1 week after treatment
sec.p:
We found renal enlargement (Fig. 3AFig. 3.
Renal morphology in ANS mice at 1 week after treatment. (A) Appearance of kidney from sham and ANS mice after 1 week of treatment. Scale bar represent 5 mm. (B) Kidney weight (n=18–20). Representative PAS staining images in (C) cortex and medulla, (D) glomeruli in kidney. Scale bars represent 200 µm in (C) and 20 µm in (D). (E) Diameter of glomeruli from 4 Sham or ANS treated mice (n=141–147). (F) Representative H&E staining images in renal vessels. Scale bars represent 20 µm. Data are shown as means ± SEM. Statistical differences were determined using Mann-Whitney U test (*, P<0.05; ****, P<0.0001 as compared to sham mice).) with increased kidney weight (Fig. 3B) in ANS group at 1 week after treatment. Histological examinations did not show structural differences in renal cortex and medulla between Sham and ANS-treated group (Fig. 3C, upper and lower-panels). Meanwhile, the growth of mesangial matrix in the glomerulus was observed in the kidney from ANS mice (Fig. 3D, dashed line section). Moreover, glomerular diameter of ANS group was significantly increased compared with that in Sham group at 1 week after ANS treatment (Fig. 3E). In addition, renal arterial walls of ANS group were thickened in ANS mice compared with those in Sham group (Fig. 3F).

We also examined the kidney function in ANS group at 1 week after treatment. Urinary albumin was significantly increased (Fig. 4AFig. 4.
Impaired renal function in ANS mice at 1 week after treatment. (A) Urinary albumin levels (n=8–10), (B) creatinine clearance (n=8–10), (C) plasma cystatin C levels (n=19–20), (D) urinary NGAL levels (n=8–10) in Sham and ANS mice at 1 week after treatment. Data are shown as means ± SEM. Statistical differences were determined using Mann-Whitney U test (*, P<0.05; **, P<0.01; ****, P<0.0001 as compared to sham mice).), and creatinine clearance was decreased (Fig. 4B). Moreover, the increased levels of plasma cystatin C (Fig. 4C) and urinary NGAL excretion (Fig. 4D) were observed in the ANS group, indicating the kidney dysfunction at 1week after ANS treatment.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The structure-function relationship of heart or kidney influences each performance to maintain body homeostasis [16]. Functional impairment of either organ, accompanied by structural alteration, initiates a cascade of events leading to and amplifying failure in both organs [17]. A critical step toward treating cardiorenal damages is to understand when heart and kidney are affected in their structure and function during early stages of the injuries. Because of a paucity of knowledge regarding distant organ dysfunction, in particular, cardiorenal damages, little information is known when heart and kidney are influenced at earliest timing during progression of the disease.

Using ANS model mice with cardiorenal damages, we previously showed severe cardiac and renal dysfunction 4 weeks after the treatment of Ang II infusion, nephrectomy, and salt loading [18, 19]. In this study, we focused on the early phase of tissue alteration and used mice at one week after ANS treatment associated with reduced body weight, high blood pressure, increased levels of water intake and urine volume (Fig. 1). We found that cardiac function is maintained owing to no change in fractional shortening at this point, although hypertrophy is observed (Fig. 2). On the other hand, we revealed that kidney dysfunction with enlarged glomeruli is evident in mice one week after ANS treatment (Fig. 3), including reduced creatinine clearance, elevated levels of urinary albumin, plasma cystatin C, and urinary NGAL (Fig. 4).

We evaluated the pathophysiology of the heart from the aspects of structure and function in a week following ANS treatment and found concentric cardiac hypertrophy and cardiac fibrosis without impairment of cardiac contractile function (Fig. 2G). In comparison with other cardiorenal damage models, various rodent models for cardiorenal damages have been reported, including myocardial infarction with unilateral nephrectomy model [19], 5/6 nephrectomy with myocardial infarction model [20], doxorubicin-induced cardiorenal toxic model [21], 5/6 nephrectomy following salt loading and administration of NO synthase inhibitor [22]. In these reports, cardiac hypertrophy and fibrosis have been observed after a long period of follow-up, early cardiac events are undefined. Few studies have evaluated cardiac pathology in the early phase after the initiation of intervention, and Dikow et al. found no cardiac hypertrophy but reduced functional performance in 1 week after intervention in cardiorenal damage induced by a combination of 5/6 nephrectomy and myocardial infarction [23]. Contrary to those results, our present model of ANS mice is unique in that they show cardiac remodeling and fibrosis as early as 1 week after the treatment but keep cardiac function. Cardiac fibrosis and hypertrophy are adaptive responses to pressure overload, leading to decreased left ventricular function and heart failure [24]. Thus, cardiac fibrosis found in the early phase of ANS mice may be one of the causes that lead to deterioration of systolic function during transition to the chronic phase.

AKI is a systemic disease which is characterized by abruptly decreased renal functions such as urine output or glomerular filtration rate [25]. AKI is a common complication not only in hospitalized patients who have heart failure incidence with a high prevalence rate, but also even in those free from previously cardiovascular comorbidities [26]. Although the heart and kidneys are coordinated in localization away from each other in the body, it was recently reported that acute renal damage leads to the subsequent remote cardiac fibrosis and dysregulation [27]. Other intriguing results from animal studies have shown that AKI directly impairs and reduces cardiac contractility examined by echocardiograms at two [28] and three days [29]. These suggest that acute impaired structure-function relationships in the kidneys at 1 week after ANS treatment in our study might contribute to the exacerbation of cardiac dysfunction in the later phase.

Interestingly, segmental sclerotic lesions, including increased mesangial matrix, were identified in some of the renal glomeruli of ANS mice at 1 week after intervention. These results suggest that ANS mice mimic benign nephrosclerosis. Benign nephrosclerosis is a chronic kidney disease condition with a background of hypertension and is generally considered to have a good prognosis. However, with increasing longevity and aging of the population worldwide, it is becoming an increasingly common cause of end-stage kidney disease. In addition, some patients are known to exhibit disease progression despite good blood pressure control, the cause of which remains unknown. Based on the results of this study, the ANS mouse may serve as a pathological model to analyze the initial pathogenesis of benign nephrosclerosis.

A clinical classification of CRS is composed of five types as follows. Primary acute and chronic heart failure are defined as CRS types 1 and 2, respectively, that lead to renal dysfunction. On the other hand, primary AKI and chronic kidney disease are classified as CRS types 3 and 4, respectively, that lead to cardiac dysfunction. Systemic dysfunction that leads to organ injury and often extends beyond the heart and kidney is described as CRS type 5. In the present study, we found that ANS mice exhibit structural abnormalities and impaired function of the kidneys prior to the decline in cardiac function. These suggested that ANS mice might be a model to elucidate the mechanisms for CRS causing transition from early to late-stage damages of cardiorenal tissues [30].

sec.title: Competing Interest
sec.p: The authors have no conflicts of interest directly relevant to the content of this article.

sec.title: Authors Contributions
sec.p: NM, JI, KN, and AF conceived and designed the research. NM, JI, and KN performed the experiments, and NM, JI, KN, JU, KY and AF analyzed the data and interpreted the results of experiments. NM, JI, and KN prepared the figures. NM, JI, KN and AF drafted the manuscript. All authors agree for publication. All persons designated as authors qualify for authorship, and all those who qualify for authorship are listed.

ack.p: We are grateful to the members of the Fukamizu Laboratory for their helpful discussions and encouragement. This study was supported by JST SPRING, Grant Number JPMJSP2124 (to NM), the Grant-in-Aid for Early-Career Scientists from JSPS (to KN, Grant 22K16210), the Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (C) (to JI, Grant 26430086), from the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology of Japan, Specific Research Grants (Takeda Research Foundation), and the Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development, AMED (to AF, JP21gm1410010, JP22gm1410010).
